title: श्री शिव अष्टमूर्ति स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
भवाय शर्वाय रुद्राय पशुपतये नमः।
उग्राय भीमाय ईशानाय महादेवाय ते नमः॥१॥

भवं जलात्मकं देवं सर्वव्यापिनमीश्वरम्।
प्रणमामि सदा भक्त्या भवरूपं महेश्वरम्॥२॥

शर्वं पृथिव्यात्मकं च धरणीधरमव्ययम्।
नमामि शर्वरूपेण स्थितं देवं सनातनम्॥३॥

रुद्रमग्न्यात्मकं वन्दे तेजोरूपं हुताशनम्।
त्रिलोकदाहकं शम्भुं रुद्ररूपं नमाम्यहम्॥४॥

उग्रं वाय्वात्मकं देवं प्राणापानविभागशः।
सर्वप्राणिगतं शम्भुं उग्ररूपं नमाम्यहम्॥५॥

भीमं चाकाशरूपेण व्योमव्यापिनमीश्वरम्।
शब्दब्रह्मस्वरूपं तं भीमरूपं नमाम्यहम्॥६॥

पशुपतिं यजमानात्मकं देवं यज्ञपुरुषमीश्वरम्।
पशूनां पतिमीशानं पशुपतिं नमाम्यहम्॥७॥

ईशानं चन्द्ररूपेण सोमात्मानं सुधाकरम्।
शीतलं सर्वलोकानामीशानं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥८॥

महादेवं सूर्यरूपं तेजसां निधिमीश्वरम्।
जगच्चक्षुर्महादेवं महादेवं नमाम्यहम्॥९॥

एवमष्टाभिरूर्तिभ्यो नमः शम्भो नमो नमः।
जलं स्थलं हुताशश्च वायुराकाशमेव च॥१०॥

यजमानश्च सोमश्च सूर्यश्चेति तव प्रभो।
अष्टमूर्तिस्वरूपेण विश्वं व्याप्य व्यवस्थितम्॥११॥

॥ इति श्री शिव अष्टमूर्ति स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
Bhavaya Sharvaya Rudraya Pashupataye Namah.
Ugraya Bhimaya Ishanaya Mahadevaya Te Namah.||1||

Bhavam Jalatmakam Devam Sarvavyapinam Ishvaram.
Pranamami Sada Bhaktya Bhavarupam Maheshvaram.||2||

Sharvam Prithivyatmakam Cha Dharanidharamavyayam.
Namami Sharvarupena Sthitam Devam Sanatanam.||3||

Rudram Agnyatmakam Vande Tejorupam Hutashanam.
Trilokadahakam Shambhum Rudrarupam Namamyaham.||4||

Ugram Vayvatmakam Devam Pranapana-Vibhagashah.
Sarvapranigatan Shambhum Ugrarupam Namamyaham.||5||

Bhimam Chakasharupena Vyomavyapinam Ishvaram.
Shabdabrahmasvarupam Tam Bhimarupam Namamyaham.||6||

Pashupatim Yajamanatmakam Devam Yajnapurusham Ishvaram.
Pashunam Patim Ishanam Pashupatim Namamyaham.||7||

Ishanam Chandrarupena Somatmanam Sudhakaram.
Shitalam Sarvaloka̅nam Ishanam Pranamamyaham.||8||

Mahadevam Suryarupam Tejasam Nidhim Ishvaram.
Jagachchakshur Mahadevam Mahadevam Namamyaham.||9||

Evam Ashtabhir Urtibhyo Namah Shambho Namo Namah.
Jalam Sthalam Hutashashcha Vayurakashameva Cha.||10||

Yajamanashcha Somashcha Suryashcheti Tava Prabho.
Ashtamurtisvarupena Vishvam Vyapya Vyavasthitam.||11||

|| Iti Shri Shiva Ashtamurti Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
भव, शर्व, रुद्र, पशुपति को नमस्कार। उग्र, भीम, ईशान और महादेव — आपको नमस्कार।

जल-स्वरूप भव देव को, जो सर्वव्यापी ईश्वर हैं, उन भवरूप महेश्वर को मैं सदा भक्ति से प्रणाम करता हूँ।

पृथ्वी-स्वरूप शर्व को, धरणीधर अव्यय देव को, शर्वरूप में स्थित सनातन देव को मैं नमन करता हूँ।

अग्नि-स्वरूप रुद्र को, तेजोरूप हुताशन को, त्रिलोक को दग्ध करने वाले शम्भु रुद्ररूप को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

वायु-स्वरूप उग्र देव को, जो प्राण-अपान रूप में विभक्त हैं, सर्व प्राणियों में विद्यमान शम्भु के उग्र रूप को नमस्कार।

आकाश-स्वरूप भीम को, व्योमव्यापी ईश्वर को, शब्दब्रह्म स्वरूप भीम रूप को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

यजमान-स्वरूप पशुपति को, यज्ञपुरुष ईश्वर को, पशुओं के पति ईशान — पशुपति को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

चन्द्र-स्वरूप ईशान को, सोम-आत्मा सुधाकर को, समस्त लोकों को शीतलता देने वाले ईशान को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ।

सूर्य-स्वरूप महादेव को, तेजों के निधि ईश्वर को, जगत के नेत्र महादेव को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

इस प्रकार आठ मूर्तियों द्वारा हे शम्भो! नमस्कार। जल, पृथ्वी, अग्नि, वायु, आकाश, यजमान, सोम और सूर्य — हे प्रभो! अष्टमूर्ति स्वरूप से विश्व को व्याप्त कर स्थित हैं।

viniyog.shloka: अस्य श्रीशिवाष्टमूर्तिस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य वशिष्ठ ऋषिः। अनुष्टुप् छन्दः। श्रीअष्टमूर्तिशिव देवता। ॐ नमः शिवाय बीजम्। अष्टमूर्तिशिवकृपा शक्तिः। विश्वरूपदर्शनं कीलकम्। श्रीशिवप्रीत्यर्थे पाठे विनियोगः॥

benefits: Reveals Lord Shiva's presence in all eight elements of nature
Purifies the five elements within the body
Invokes cosmic awareness and oneness with the universe
Traditionally recited for removal of elemental imbalances and brings health
Invokes the blessing of deep philosophical understanding of shaiva siddhanta
Powerful for meditation and Tattva Shuddhi practices
description: Shiva Ashtamurti Stotram (श्री शिव अष्टमूर्ति स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shiva. Rooted in Shaiva Agama Texts, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to bestow deep philosophical understanding of Shaiva Siddhanta as well as purify the five elements within the body. Additionally, it is known to reveal Lord Shiva's presence in all eight elements of nature. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Pradosh for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 11 verses, it can be completed in approximately 7 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Ashtamurti Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiva Ashtamurti Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Prayer to eight cosmic forms of Shiva — water, earth, fire, air, space, sun, moon, and soul.
relatedSearches: Shiva Ashtamurti eight forms Sharva Bhava Rudra
Sharva Bhava Rudra Ugra Bhima Pashupati Ishana Mahadeva

seoFaqs.question: What are the eight forms enumerated in the Shiva Ashtamurti Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The classical enumeration, drawn from the Shata Rudriya section of the Yajurveda and the Kaushitaki Brahmana, lists the eight forms as Sharva (earth), Bhava (water), Rudra (fire), Ugra (air), Bhima (ether or space), Pashupati (the yajamana or sacrificer-soul), Ishana (sun) and Mahadeva (moon). Each name corresponds to one of the cosmic substances or principles through which Shiva pervades the universe. The Wikipedia entry on Astamurti, the Wisdomlib lexicon and the Shaivam.org Ashtamurtam article all reproduce this canonical octad. Some Puranic enumerations substitute 'jiva' (individual soul) for Pashupati and 'medicine/herb' for one of the elements, but the eight-name Shata Rudriya scheme is treated as the canonical reference for the Ashtamurti Stotram.

seoFaqs.question: What is the textual source of the Ashtamurti Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The doctrinal foundation lies in the Yajurveda's Shata Rudriya (the Rudram), specifically passages where Rudra is praised through eight elemental epithets. Kalidasa's Raghuvamsha opens with a celebrated verse invoking Shiva as Ashtamurti, demonstrating that the eight-form theology was already a literary commonplace in classical Sanskrit by the 5th century CE. The standalone hymn titled 'Ashtamurti Stava' or 'Ashtamurti Stotra' that circulates in modern devotional collections is a later compilation drawing on these Vedic and classical sources, sometimes attributed in regional traditions to Kalidasa or to a Shankara-era acharya. The text's authority rests on its alignment with the Shata Rudriya rather than on a single attributed author.

seoFaqs.question: Why is the Ashtamurti scheme theologically important in Shaivism?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ashtamurti scheme expresses the core Shaiva claim that Shiva is not merely one deity among many but the totality of cosmic principles. By identifying Shiva with the five elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether), the two luminaries (sun, moon) and the embodied soul (jiva or yajamana), the doctrine asserts that everything one perceives or experiences is already a form of Shiva. This is the Shaiva counterpart to Vaishnava vishvarupa theology and provides the metaphysical basis for the practice of seeing Shiva in nature, in oneself and in all worship. The stotram, by enumerating these eight forms in praise, performs the daily reaffirmation of this cosmological claim and trains the devotee to perceive Shiva pervasively rather than locally.

seoFaqs.question: How are the eight forms mapped to the cardinal and intermediate directions?
seoFaqs.answer: Several Shaiva sources, including the Aapkikundli and Wisdomlib summaries, preserve a directional mapping in which Sharva (earth) governs the East, Bhava (water) the Northeast, Rudra (fire) the North, Ugra (air) the Northwest, Bhima (ether) the West, Pashupati (hidden form) the Southwest, Ishana (sun) the South, and Mahadeva (moon) the Southeast. This eight-direction scheme is used during ashtamurti puja for setting up the sacred space, with offerings made in the prescribed directions. Temple architecture in some Shaiva shrines, especially in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, places eight murti niches around the central lingam, reflecting this scheme. The Ashtamurti Stotram is then chanted while making circumambulation through the eight directions.

seoFaqs.question: When and how is the Ashtamurti Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The hymn is recited daily as part of nitya-paath in Smarta and Shaiva Siddhanta households, on Mondays and Pradosham evenings, during Shravana and Karthika months, and on Maha Shivaratri. It is also chanted by priests at the start of Rudra-abhishekam to invoke Shiva's eight cosmic forms before the elemental ablutions begin. In contemplative practice, advanced sadhakas pair each verse with a meditation on the corresponding element, training awareness to recognise Shiva in each principle. The standard posture is seated facing east or north before a Shiva lingam, after a bath, with vibhuti applied. Pronunciation of the eight names with their proper Vedic accents is encouraged, ideally learned from a competent priest or recording.

seoFaqs.question: Does the Ashtamurti Stotram have a phala-shruti (promised fruits) verse?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes, most published recensions include a closing phala-shruti verse declaring that the devotee who recites the Ashtamurti Stotram daily attains harmony with the eight cosmic forms, freedom from elemental afflictions (such as illnesses born of imbalance among the five elements), longevity, prosperity and ultimately union with Shiva. The phala is framed in cosmological rather than petitionary terms because the practice itself is a re-attunement to the cosmic order. Shaivam.org and Mukhopadhyay's Ashtamurti article explicitly note that the recitation is recommended as a remedy for elemental disharmonies in life such as recurring fevers (fire-related), fluid retention (water-related) or restlessness (air-related), reflecting the bhuta-shuddhi (element-purification) function of the hymn.
